senses.gloss: a blow, a stroke, a hit
a shot, a report of a gun

etymology.text: Instance noun from ضَرَبَ (ḍaraba, “to beat, hit”).

body.text: [signifies the same in an intensive sense, i. e.
or in a frequentative sense, i. e.
is used in relation to several, or many, objects, as will be shown in what follows]: (K:) accord. to Er-Rághib,
the making a thing to fall upon another thing;
the making it to fall with violence,
], i. e. with a sword, (A, Mgh, Msb), &c. (A, Msb.) And
I struck Zeyd with a whip
He struck him a hundred strokes of the whip

action] or multiplicity [of the objects]: AZ says that, when the object is one, the Arabs use only the former verb, without teshdeed; but when there is a plurality of objects, either of the verbs; (Msb;) [so that] one says,
He gave the order to smite the necks,
in the Kur xlvii. 4 is originally
Then do ye smite the necks,
]; (Bd;) the inf. n. being here put for its verb. (Jel.) [Respecting the phrase
.] ―         -b2-  [Hence a variety of meanings and phrases here following.] ―         -b3-
trained, his dog for the purpose of the chase
Camels shall not be ridden, save to three mosques:
[namely, that of Mekkeh, that of El-Medeeneh, and that of El-Aksà at Jerusalem:] a trad. (TA. [See also 4 in art.
see expl. in the latter half of this paragraph.] ―         -b6-
I struck the string of the bow with the wooden implement
He struck the chords of the lute;
, aor. and inf. n. as above,
stake, so that it became firm in the ground.
He pitched the tent, by knocking in its pegs with a mallet:
, aor. and inf. n. as above, (tropical:)
applied, the mud upon the wall,
as a plaster]. (TA.) ―         -b12-  Hence, accord. to some, the phrase
, in the Kur ii. 58, considered as meaning (assumed tropical:)
Vileness was made to cleave to them:
like as the tent encompasses him over whom it is pitched. (Ksh, Bd.) And [in like manner] one says,
He imposed upon the slave the tax according to a fixed time.
The being sent to the war was appointed them and imposed upon them as an obligation.
He cast the net over the bird:
The snare was cast over the bird
The night cast its folds of darkness;
as appears from the following verse.] Homeyd says,

saraY mivola naboDi AlEiroqi waAll~ayolu DaAribN
                      
                     
                        biA^rowaAqihi waAlS~uboHu qado kaAda yasoTaEu
He went on in his night-journey, like the pulsing of the vein, while the night was casting its folds of darkness
and the dawn had almost risen
A barrier was set between them two
(K, TA;) as though by putting a covering over their ears; a metonymical [and elliptical] mode of saying
by preventing any sound from penetrating into their ears, in consequence of which they would have awoke: (Zj, L, TA:) or
he poured upon them sleep so that they slept and did not awake:
I poured sleep upon him by closing his ear
, (A, K, * TA,) aor. and inf. n. as above, (TA,) (tropical:)
The cold smote it so as to injure it;
namely, herbage; and in like manner one says of the wind: (IKtt, TA:) and ↓
The cold smote it by its vehemence, so that it dried up;
and in like manner one says of the wind: (TA:) and
rime, fell upon the land, so that its herbage became nipped,
He was smitten with a trial,

body.text_bw: Tariyqu mak~apa maA DarabahaA AlEaAma qaTorapN
body.text_ar: طَرِيقُ مَكَّةَ مَا ضَرَبَهَا العَامَ قَطْرَةٌ

body.text: The road to Mekkeh, not a drop
has fallen upon it this year
also, accord. to Fr, but this latter, though agreeable with analogy, is disallowed by Sb and Akh, (TA,) (tropical:)
The hire of the camel's leaping the female:
the taking of which, as also the taking of the hire of any stallion for covering, is forbidden in a trad. (TA.) ―         -b19-
: (TA:) accord. to some, said peculiarly in relation to milk; (MF, TA;) but [SM says,] this I have not found in any lexicon. (TA.)
He collected the milk in the skin, and poured fresh milk upon that which was curdled,
he poured the milk into the skin, and kept it therein that its butter might come forth
]. (A.) In the L and other lexicons it is said that
I caused them to become confused
has a similar meaning: see 2.]) And
goat, was intermixed with such a colour.
The trees struck their roots into the earth

body.text_ar: ضَرَبَتْ فِيهِ فُلَانَةُ بِعِرْقٍ ذِى اَشَبٍ

body.text: ; (S and TA in the present art., and in like manner, in both, in art.
hereditary, quality, of a dubious kind:
relates to a family, or people; for it is said that] the meaning is,
such a woman corrupted their race by her bringing forth among them:
, or, accord. to more common usage,
]. (TA. [This saying is also mentioned in the A, as tropical, but is not expl. therein.]) ―         -b22-
He played with the gamingarrows; practised sortilege with arrows,
I practised sortilege with the people,
He practised sortilege with the two arrows;

one of which was inscribed with the sentence “ My Lord hath commanded me, ” and the other with “ My Lord hath forbidden me: ” a person between hope and despair is likened to one practising this mode of sortilege, which was used by the people of the Time of Ignorance when they doubted whether they should undertake an affair or abstain from it. (Har pp. 465 and 553.) One says also,
(Mgh.) And hence the saying of El-Hareeree,
And I obtained a share of its pasture
He shall take thereof somewhat, according to what is due to him, of the third part.
]: and thus is expl. the saying of Aboo-Haneefeh,
of more than the third part;
the true objective complement being suppressed. (Mgh.) ―         -b23-
He swung his arms, and moved them about, in his manner of walking
being understood after the verb] (assumed tropical:)
pointed, with his hand, towards a thing.
He put forth his hand towards such a thing, to take it,
He applied his hand to the doing of such a thing
a phrase expl. to me by IbrD as meaning (assumed tropical:)
He busied his hands with the property,
in the giving, or dispensing of it.] ―         -b25-
for it is a custom, when two persons are bargaining together, for one of them to put his hand upon the other's in ratifying the bargain. (TA, from a trad.) ―         -b26-  And (tropical:)
hindered, him, from doing a thing,
from doing a thing that he had begun:
(K, TA.) And (i. e. the former phrase) (tropical:)
disposing of, his property according to his own free will.
signifies, (S, Msb,) or signifies also, (TA,) and (Msb, TA) so does
He turned away from, avoided, shunned,
(S *  Msb, K * TA; *) namely, a person, (TA,) or a thing. (Msb.)
, in the Kur [xliii. 4], is said to mean (assumed tropical:)
Shall we then neglect you, and not teach you what is incumbent on you?
the phrase being taken from a rider's striking his beast with his stick when he desires to turn him from the course that he is pursuing: or the meaning is, (assumed tropical:)
shall we then turn away the Kur-án from you,
and not invite you thereby to the faith,
I turned away from him and left him.
: see 1 in that art.) See also the saying
He smote with himself the ground;
app. often used in this sense; (a phrase similar to
(AZ, ISk, S, K, TA) as used in the phrase
. [See also other explanations of this last phrase in a later part of this paragraph.]) And
in such a place of alighting.
in a place by the water
satisfied themselves with drinking and then lay down around the water
by the watering-troughs, to be brought again to drink another time:
The people's camels satisfied themselves with drinking until they lay down and remained in their place
the people satisfied their thirst and then abode at the water.
He beats for it the whole land,
i. e. in journeying,] means (assumed tropical:)
he seeks it through the whole land:
so says AZ in explanation of the phrase here following. (O, TA.)
he applies himself with art and diligence to gain glory,
and seeks it through the whole land.
according to the model, make, fashion,
This is his nature, with an adaptation,
a disposition, to which he was moulded,
declared, a parable, a similitude, an example,
said of God [and of a man]: (S, * O, * Msb, TA:) or
thus expl., the verb has but one objective complement: or the phrase signifies
&c.; and so has two objective complements: in the saying in the Kur [xxxvi. 12]
And propound thou to them a parable, the people of the town
] i. e., the story of the people of the town, [or
make thou to them a parable,
an example, the people of the town;
may be in the accus. case as an objective complement,

may be regarded as a second objective complement [i. e. second in the order of the words, but first in the order of the sense]: the phrase is differently expl. on account of the different meanings of the verb
&c.; accord. to some, it is taken from the phrase
[q. v.]; because of the impression which a parable or the like makes upon the mind: accord. to some, from
signifying “ a like; ” because the first thing is made like the second: accord. to some, from
[q. v.; because the mud, applied as a plaster, conforms to the shape of the wall]: and accord. to some, from
[q. v.]; because of the correspondence between a parable or the like and the object to which it is applied, and the correspondence between the signet and its impression. (TA, from the M and L &c.)
it, a subject of a parable, a similitude, an example,
is applied to, in relation to,
to the case of, such a thing
. (MA. [App. from a phrase in the Kur xx. 79, q. v.]) ―         -b38-
as a conventional term of the accountants, or arithmeticians, means The
a number by another number; (Mgh, Msb;) as when you say, [
[is often intrans., and thus] signifies also
became, in a state of commotion,
&c.]: (K:) [see also 8, which is more commonly used in this sense:] or,
became, in a state of strong commotion.
, (A, K,) or, as in some lexicons,
raised her tail, and smote her vulva with it,
took fright, and ran away at random,
and ceased not to gallop and leap until he had thrown off all his furniture,
(A.) It is said in a trad. of 'Alee, When such and such things shall happen, (mentioning faction, or sedition, or the like,)
The leader of the religion shall hasten to go away through the land,
fleeing from the faction, or sedition: or, as some say,
shall go away hastily through the land, with his followers.
, as shown above, has a similar meaning:] or (tropical:)
he went forth in the land as a merchant;
he arose, and hastened in his journey through the land:
(TA.) The verb is [similarly] used in relation to almost all employments: you say,
He travelled for the purpose of traffic
Verily I have to make a journey for the sake of,
on account of, a thousand dirhems
]. (S, TA: but in my copies of the S,
the time pursued a part of its course;
a part of the time passed.

body.text_bw: Daraba Ald~ahoru mino DarabaAnihi A^ano kaAna ka*aA waka*aA
body.text_ar: ضَرَبَ الدَّهْرُ مِنْ ضَرَبَانِهِ اَنْ كَانَ كَذَا وَكَذَا

body.text: time, brought to pass, among its events, that such and such things happened
made a wide separation between us;
it was smitten by the cold,
The land was smitten by hoar-frost,
rime, and its herbage was nipped,
A single act of beating, striking,

body.text_bw: laA A=xu*u maAliY Ealayoka A_il~aA DarobapF waAHidapF
body.text_ar: لَا ا=خُذُ مَالِى عَلَيْكَ اـِلَّا ضَرْبَةً وَاحِدَةً

body.text: I will not take what is due to me on thy part save at one time,
The saying of the diver for pearls, to the merchant, I will dive for thee once, and what I shall bring up shall be thine for such a price.
